purpose: The canonical accessor layer for dashboard directory layout and on-disk state. Owns getDashboardDir(id) (path joining), ensureDashboard(id) (idempotent dir + default JSON bootstrap), sync+async readers for initialization.json/logs.json/progress/*.json, clearDashboardProgress(id) (deletes progress files only), listDashboards() (returns sorted dashboards/* dirs that have an initialization.json — the validity gate), nextDashboardId() (3-byte hex via crypto.randomBytes with collision retry against listDashboards), copyDirSync (used by ArchiveService for archive copies), deleteDashboard (recursive rmSync), getDashboardCreationTime (birthtime/ctime fallback), and getFullDashboardState (composite read). All other server services build on top of this module — it is the abstraction boundary between filesystem and consumers.
gotchas: listDashboards is the de facto validity gate — a dashboard directory without initialization.json is invisible to the entire system (watchers, archive flow, dispatch, IPC list endpoints). Manually creating a dashboards/{id}/ folder without seeding initialization.json silently makes it 'not exist' from the server's perspective.

ensureDashboard seeds initialization.json with DEFAULT_INITIALIZATION via writeAtomic. If the file is later truncated/corrupted, ensureDashboard does NOT repair it (existsSync guard) — bad state persists across server restarts until manually cleared.

nextDashboardId loops until it finds a non-colliding id. With 6-hex ids (16M space) collisions are rare, but the loop has no upper bound — if listDashboards somehow returned the entire space (e.g., a misconfigured DASHBOARDS_DIR pointing at /tmp), this would hang.

readDashboardProgress silently swallows all errors with `catch { /* ignore */ }` — corrupt progress files do not surface as errors; they just don't appear in the returned map. WatcherService is the only place schema errors are logged.

copyDirSync is used by ArchiveService and is sync — copying a large dashboard with many progress files blocks the event loop for the duration. Acceptable today (dashboards are small) but a scaling cliff.

getDashboardCreationTime falls back to ctimeMs on systems without birthtime (some Linux fs), and to Infinity on stat failure — Infinity sorts to end-of-list, which is the intentional 'unknown means newest' behavior. Don't 'fix' this to 0 (would invert ordering).

deleteDashboard uses fs.rmSync({recursive:true,force:true}) — destroys all progress, logs, and history sources for that id with no recovery. Always called from ArchiveService archive flow OR explicit user reset; never call directly from a route without a confirmation gate.
patterns: Sync + Async parallel pairs (readDashboardInit / readDashboardInitAsync etc.) — sync used in tight loops (watcher reconciliation, listDashboards) where blocking I/O is acceptable; async used by IPC handler aggregations.
Atomic write pattern via utils/json.writeAtomic — used for default file seeding to prevent half-written defaults if the process crashes during ensureDashboard.
Validity-gate pattern: listDashboards() filters by `existsSync(initialization.json)` — used elsewhere as 'this dashboard is real'.
Object-keyed-by-task_id return shape for progress: callers (DependencyService, HistoryService, WatcherService) all assume `progressMap[task_id]` lookup.
conventions: Module is functional (no class), exports a flat object of named functions.
All sync functions swallow filesystem errors and return null/empty defaults — never throw to caller.
Path construction always via path.join with constants — never string concat.
Async variants only catch readdir/stat failures (`catch { /* dir may not exist */ }`) and return empty defaults.
